questions.question: What sound does the raven make?
questions.explanation: According to the text, ravens also croak like frogs.

questions.question: Which animal is shown to be cunning in stories?
questions.explanation: The fox is depicted as clever and cunning in the poem.

questions.question: What does the fox call the raven?
questions.explanation: The fox praises the raven's appearance, calling it a 'handsome bird'.

questions.question: Why did the raven drop the morsel?
questions.explanation: The raven forgot that it only croaked and opened its beak in pride.

questions.question: What lesson does the fox teach the raven?
questions.explanation: The fox teaches the raven not to be too proud of itself.

questions.question: Who is Reynard in the poem?
questions.explanation: Reynard is the name given to the fox in the poem.

questions.question: What did Mr. Raven hold in his beak?
questions.explanation: The raven held a piece of food, called a 'morsel', in its beak.

questions.question: Where do the birds want to make the raven king?
questions.explanation: The poem mentions that if the raven sang, it would be called king by the birds of the woods.

questions.question: What does Reynard look up at in stanza 1?
questions.explanation: Reynard looks up at Mr. Raven perched on a limb.

questions.question: Who laughs after the raven drops the morsel?
questions.explanation: The fox, Reynard, laughs and says 'Ha-ha' after the raven drops the food.

notes.title: Main Characters
notes.content: The two main characters are Mr. Raven and Reynard the Fox.

notes.title: Poetic Devices
notes.content: The poem uses poetic devices such as metaphors and similes to create a vivid picture.

notes.title: Moral Lesson
notes.content: The story teaches a moral lesson about the dangers of pride and being too confident.

notes.title: Imagery
notes.content: The poem uses imagery to describe the scene, such as 'such feathers' and 'the woods'.

notes.title: Character Development
notes.content: Both Mr. Raven and Reynard undergo character development throughout the story.

notes.title: Plot Structure
notes.content: The poem follows a simple plot structure, with an introduction, rising action, climax, and resolution.

notes.title: Tone
notes.content: The tone of the poem is lighthearted and humorous, with a touch of satire.

notes.title: Setting
notes.content: The story takes place in a forest or woods, where birds live.

notes.title: Theme
notes.content: The theme of the poem is the importance of humility and not letting pride get in the way.

notes.title: Author's Message
notes.content: The author, Jean de la Fontaine, is trying to convey a message about the dangers of pride through this fable.

facts.fact: Ravens and frogs both croak sounds similar to each other.
Reynard is an old French name for the fox, which was later translated into English as Fox.
Jean de la Fontaine was a French poet who wrote many fables in the 17th century.
The poem uses the device of personification to describe the raven's voice as 'just a croak'.
Mr. Raven is shown to be foolishly proud, which leads to his downfall.
Reynard teaches Mr. Raven that it's not wise to let pride get in the way of being humble and kind.
The poem has a rhyming scheme and is written in quatrains, with four lines per stanza.
The story uses satire to poke fun at human nature and societal norms.
Jean de la Fontaine was known for his use of animal characters to convey moral lessons.
The poem's title, 'Mr. Raven', is likely a reference to the bird's name in the story.
